From large language models like ChatGPT to frontier AI agents, these technologies demonstrate immense potential in productivity, creativity and problem-solving capability. Conversely, AI poses serious challenges in safety and ethics, in which deepfakes are a significant concern. Deepfake refers to synthetic media, typically videos, audio, or images, generated using deep learning algorithms or AI to create realistic digital media or alterations (time.com). It could resemble a person’s voice and face, faking behaviors that they actually never did. Due to the improving sophistication of algorithms, it has become increasingly difficult to discern deepfake videos from authentic content, causing severe misinformation. This report focuses on a particular form of deepfake exploitation – political deepfakes, deriving whether they should be forcefully detected on removed on social media platforms. It considers various global and national perspectives and includes the author’s own view on solutions to the issue.Global Perspective:(Issue: vast presence over many countries globally)	Globally, some believe political deepfakes are a major issue that requires active regulation. In India, political deepfakes were vastly exploited to smear opponents. The Indian National Congress, the country’s major opposition party committed the largest spread of deepfakes on social media. One example is in April 2024, INC published deepfake videos of Bollywood actors criticizing India’s governors. Furthermore, a video of INC politician and an opposition leader being sworn in was layered with AI-generated audio, as though the leader resigned from the party. (Mani) Another major manipulation of political deepfakes involves achieving more votes during election periods. Politicians easily reached remote voters from around the country, by creating targeted deepfake phone calls to persuade residents to vote (Mani).  Certain unfair advantage created via deepfake technologies broke the balance of democratic processes, disrupted normal functioning of the local government, and lessened integrity and public trust. Consequently, the Indian Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) drafted laws to require social media platforms to detect and label deepfake content, actively resisting political misinformation and unfairness.	In the United States, AI-generated political content also exists in substantial quantities. The Republican National Committee (RNC) posted a deepfake advertisement to attack Joe Biden for his election in 2023, to urge the public not to vote for Biden. The video involves a Chinese invasion of Taiwan (image of a scary Chinese fighter plane), financial markets in "free fall," etc. (Pierson), attempting to blacken political reputation of an elector. Another deepfake posted on Trump's Truth Social account depicts Florida Governor Ron DeSantis riding a rhinoceros to denote "RINO," i.e., "Republican In Name Only." (Pierson) 	On the other hand, another perspective highlights the limited negative impact of deepfakes. One data analysis on the worldwide political elections in 2024 points out that AI-generated content accounted for less than 1% of all fact-checked disinformation (Clegg). Another study reports similar results that deepfake can deceive American public (nearly 50% in the experiment sample), but the scandal is no more so than equivalent misinformation of innovative formats, such as textual headlines or audio recordings (Barari et al.). Babari and his colleagues also suggest that broad public literacy in politics and informational technology most effectively improve discernment of deepfake videos of political elites. In India, large quantities of AI-generated videos on social media were once described as “cheapfakes” (Mani),  reflecting low costs to produce these contents and their low quality. Hence, it is possible to discern between deepfakes and authentic videos by observing and training. Sound syncing, facial movements, lighting, and language uses are aspects to effectively distinguish AI-generated content (Rascius). By promoting public awareness, people are more likely to recognize and distrust political deepfakes and halt misinformation due to AI’s seemingly sophisticated algorithms. National Perspective:Local Perspective:Causes:Consequences:(Social media platforms claim that they restrict deepfakes. From large language models like ChatGPT to frontier AI agents, these technologies demonstrate immense potential in productivity, creativity and problem-solving capability. According to the latest report, ChatGPT has over 700 million weekly active users (OpenAI), ranging from data analysts to financiers. Conversely, AI poses serious challenges in safety and ethics, in which deepfakes are a significant concern. Deepfake refers to synthetic media, typically videos, audio, or images, generated using deep learning algorithms or AI to create realistic digital media or alterations (“The Definition of Deepfake.”). It could resemble a person’s voice and face, faking behaviors that they actually never did. Due to the improving sophistication of algorithms, it has become increasingly difficult to discern deepfake videos from authentic content, causing severe misinformation. This report focuses on a particular form of deepfake exploitation – political deepfakes, deriving whether they should be forcefully detected on removed on social media platforms. It considers various global and national perspectives and includes the author’s own view on solutions to the issue.Global Perspective:	Globally, some believe political deepfakes are a major issue that requires active regulation. In India, political deepfakes were vastly exploited to smear opponents. The Indian National Congress, the country’s major opposition party committed the largest spread of deepfakes on social media. One example is in April 2024, INC published deepfake videos of Bollywood actors criticizing India’s governors. Furthermore, a video of INC politician and an opposition leader being sworn in was layered with AI-generated audio, as though the leader resigned from the party. (Mani) Another major manipulation of political deepfakes involves achieving more votes during election periods. Politicians easily reached remote voters from around the country, by creating targeted deepfake phone calls to persuade residents to vote (Mani).  Certain unfair advantage created via deepfake technologies broke the balance of democratic processes, disrupted normal functioning of the local government, and lessened integrity and public trust. Consequently, the Indian Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) drafted laws to require social media platforms to detect and label deepfake content, actively resisting political misinformation and unfairness.	In United States, AI-generated political content also exists in substantial quantities. The Republican National Committee (RNC) posted a deepfake advertisement to attack Joe Biden for his election in 2023, to urge the public not to vote for Biden. The video involves a Chinese invasion of Taiwan, financial markets in "free fall," etc. (Pierson), attempting to blacken political reputation of an elector. Another deepfake posted on Trump's Truth Social account depicts Florida Governor Ron DeSantis riding a rhinoceros to denote "RINO," i.e., "Republican In Name Only." (Pierson) The regulation of deepfakes in United States focuses on election fairness and personal privacy (Wang). Policies restricting AI-generated content are diverged among different states. California bans deepfakes in elections, while Virginia punishes their unauthorized dissemination (Wang). The government also accredits technology companies like Google and Microsoft to control and constrain AI risks (Wang). This tendency shows the US government concerned relatively less about deepfakes.Global social media platforms claim that they restrict deepfakes. Facebook and Youtube have announced to remove deepfakes; Twitter, or X, labels deepfakes and prohibits sharing of "misleading media" which "deceive or confuse people and lead to harm." (Pierson)	On the other hand, another perspective highlights the limited negative impact of deepfakes. One data analysis on the worldwide political elections in 2024 points out that AI-generated content accounted for less than 1% of all fact-checked disinformation (Clegg). Another experimental study reports similar results that deepfake can deceive American public (nearly 50% in the experiment sample), but the scandal is no more so than equivalent misinformation of innovative formats, such as fake news and audios (Barari et al.). Barari and his colleagues also suggest that broad public literacy in politics and informational technology most effectively improve discernment of deepfake videos of political elites. In India, large quantities of AI-generated videos on social media were once described as “cheapfakes” (Mani), reflecting low costs to produce these contents and their low quality. Hence, it is possible to discern between deepfakes and authentic videos by observing and training. Sound syncing, facial movements, lighting, and language use are aspects to effectively distinguish AI-generated content (Rascius). By promoting public awareness, people are more likely to recognize and distrust political deepfakes and halt the misinformation.National and Local Perspective:In China, the government prioritizes detection of deepfakes mainly for security concerns. Compared to policies in United States, the Chinese government worn a more serious attitude towards deepfakes with stricter laws. President Xi recognized the tendency in AI’s development and urged drafting laws and policies regarding AI’s application and ethical considerations, ensuring reliable, stable development of technology and artificial intelligence (Zhao). Online fraudulence remained high while some frauds involved manipulating deepfakes in online transactions, defrauding victims of over 2 million RMB (Wang). Departments like the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) drafted laws and policy to restrict spread of AI-generated misinformation. In 2021, CAC took “regulatory measures on social networking software to address risks caused by deepfake technology.” (Wang) For example, a large number of deepfake videos on Douyin were discovered and removed by CAC, and related parties were punished. The policy “Implementation Outline for a Rule of Law Society (2020-2025)” emphasized regulation on AI media, and the draft “Data Security Management Measure” made labeling deepfakes on social media a mandatory requirement (Wang). Causes:	The fact that (political) deepfakes hold great scale of impact on global citizens can be ascribed to several causes.	First, technological advancements in artificial intelligence propelled the rise of deepfakes. In 2016, deepfake content became known since deep learning technology evolved; and the success of GAN technology made image generation elementary (Wang). One described deepfakes, “they have been democratized.” (Mani) Commercial and even complimentary deepfake services emerged in the market (Liu). Examples include AI image and video generators like DALLE and Sora 2 from OpenAI, and the newly released Seedance 2.0 pushed further the convenience of deepfake creation. All required is a simple prompt, people have the opportunity to easily generate and publish their own deepfakes. 	Second, social media enable quick flows of information transmission. Compared to traditional newspapers, the Internet simplifies the process of sharing information – a post on X, or a short video on YouTube could soon achieve great focus. As deepfakes popularized, digital platforms paved the path to spread these AI-generated content. Therefore, circulation of deepfakes among the public might be attributed to Internet development.	Third, uneven and inadequate public cognition on deepfake plays a key part in its wide-spread and influence. Lacking knowledge in the frontmost deepfake technology makes it difficult to distinguish real videos and AI-generated content, tempting people to blindly credit misinformation and misunderstand political elites (Barari, et. al.). Consequences:	Wide spread of political deepfakes could lead to several consequences on countries and societies.	Firstly, political deepfakes break the balance of democratic elections. During election periods, partisan residents could spread deepfakes to criticize and disgrace political leaders. A deepfake of “Biden in a wheelchair” (Pierson) was made to suggest Biden was too old for becoming a president; Donald Trump under arrest (Pierson) was another example. Certain disinformation could mystify the public to some extent, possibly causing shifts in public views on election. Besides citizens, officials also committed manipulating deepfakes to gain advantage in elections. Indian politicians utilized deepfake phone calls, advertising people to vote for them. Hence, deepfake has effect of breaking election equity through manipulation of public opinions.	Secondly, political deepfakes weaken trust and exacerbate conflicts. In 2019 when conflict between India and Pakistan rose, deepfake videos involving demonstrations and marches by citizens from both countries, which irritated local nationalists and deteriorated international relationships (Liu). Both countries were obliged to temporarily close some social media platforms, preventing further amplification of turbulence.  Such social unrest caused by deepfakes represented how political deepfakes worsened relationship between nations.Courses of Action:To minimize the consequences of political deepfakes, two courses of actions are considered.First, governments should release policies and laws to restrain circulation of political deepfakes. Especially for instances in countries like India, where AI content significantly affected election fairness, local government could strictly implement rules to completely ban the exploitation of deepfake medias on national politics. The government could require digital platforms to detect deepfakes and possess rights to take down AI-generated content that breaks equity of democratic processes, therefore effectively reducing the impact of political deepfakes. However, this solution has potential drawback in its practicality. The government executes laws with too much force, in this case, mandatorily requiring digital platforms to detect and comprehensively eliminate the presence of deepfakes. Social platforms tend to reject coordination with the government and are possibly banned or restricted by the country. Hence, forceful policies couldn’t be applied on all social medias, so they aren’t the best choice to fight against political deepfakes. Another approach for governments resembles the deepfake policy published in China, “Implementation Outline for a Rule of Society,” which involves forcing to detect and label deepfakes on social media. This method is more practical since prevalent video platforms like Douyin (Wang) and BiliBili distinctly labeled AI-generated content, while national politics-related faked content isn’t permitted to be published, which prohibits impact of political deepfakes.  (The United Nation could also engage in drafting policies …)Second, enhancing public literacy on artificial intelligence also contributes to lessened influence of political deepfakes. Direct instructions are encouraged, such as lectures and meetings hosted in communities, or posters and advertisements introducing the concept of AI-generated content. Residents are informed by experts or experienced people about the typical characteristics of deepfakes, like unnatural facial movements and speaking tone (Rascius). As a result, people within local communities acquire skills and experience on distinguishing deepfakes and deceptions are less likely to appear. Certain method is less time-efficient and cost-effective. Making public speeches in town requires excessive labor and expenditure like inviting speakers and also occupies residents’ spare time. Consequently, direct instructions aren’t enough practical. An alternative approach is to prompt short videos that offer concise introduction about practical methods to discern between deepfakes and real information. Through social media, global citizens could actively consider and learn about the world’s newest technology by themselves, increasing awareness on misinformation from deepfakes. It’s more likely to observe changes from such approach, because digital platforms provide the medium for rapid information transfer, especially for short videos, so that more people see and learn via the Internet. Moreover, this method is less expensive as creating and watching such informational video takes short amounts of time only. Integrating the two courses of action most effectively reduces the impact of political deepfakes.  Inhibition of AI content on digital platforms provides insurance on its widespread, and improved literacy to discern deepfakes decreases misinformation on politics (Barari et. al). Conclusion:In conclusion, political deepfakes are an important global issue that requires active regulation, as they impact democratic practices and lowers trust among the public. Apart from technological detection of AI content, it is also important to foster public literacy and knowledge in political and digital technology. In China, deepfake detection is strictly prioritized for national security, while in United States, regulation of deepfakes focuses on election fairness and isn’t unified as a whole country. Caused by technological advancements and driven by social media platforms, political deepfakes need both strict restriction and improved public literacy in politics and digital technology.Personal Perspective:	The evidence and sources used in this report are mostly reliable and support the perspectives effectively. I referenced a formal academic research paper written by Barari and his colleagues. In the study, they conducted multiple experiments that applied detailed experimental techniques and made the examinations credible using statistical data. For example, “a nationally representative sample of 5,750 respondents” were included for the survey result, and two experiments, namely, “Exposure experiment” and “Detection experiment” were clearly recorded. This piece of evidence strongly supported one major global perspective that increased public literacy is considerable to limit the impact of deepfakes, because deepfakes affected no more than “equivalent misinformation conveyed through existing news formats.” I also used information from Wang’s paper which compared deepfake policies in United States and China. It was a credible academic journal source that provided formal citations. For instance, the author mentioned, “GAN technology made significant progress,” and referenced another published academic journal. The description of policies provided an objective view and elaborated my research by contrasting China and United States’ different national perspectives on how they chose to regulate deepfakes. Another source (Zhao) is published on Xinhua (news.cn), a influential news source in China that is responsible for posting authorized governmental news for the Communist Party of China and the country (基本情况_新华社).  Therefore, I regarded this source as reliable and suitable for my report. This news represented the national view from President Xi on the development of artificial intelligence, that the country considered ethics and discipline on technological innovation, supporting the strict policies related to deepfakes introduced later in the report. Furthermore, the evidence on India’s democratic election was a political review published on journal websites of Harvard University. The comment provided detailed information on the status quo of deepfakes manipulation during election in India with vivid examples. However, a limitation is that the journal potentially contained personal and biased political value, so perspectives from this source should be considered critically. Hence, I selected objective facts but avoided personal views.

Online fraudulence remained high while some frauds involved manipulating deepfakes in online transactions, defrauding victims of over 2 million RMB (Wang). Departments like the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) drafted laws and policy to restrict spread of AI-generated misinformation. In 2021, CAC took “regulatory measures on social networking software to address risks caused by deepfake technology.” (Wang) For example, a large number of deepfake videos on Douyin were discovered and removed by CAC, and related parties were punished. The policy “Implementation Outline for a Rule of Law Society (2020-2025)” emphasized regulation on AI media, and the draft “Data Security Management Measure” made labeling deepfakes on social media a mandatory requirement (Wang). Causes:	The fact that (political) deepfakes hold great scale of impact on global citizens can be ascribed to several causes.	First, technological advancements in artificial intelligence propelled the rise of deepfakes. In 2016, deepfake content became known since deep learning technology evolved; and the success of GAN technology made image generation elementary (Wang). One described deepfakes, “they have been democratized.” (Mani) Commercial and even complimentary deepfake services emerged in the market (Liu). Examples include AI image and video generators like DALLE and Sora 2 from OpenAI, and the newly released Seedance 2.0 pushed further the convenience of deepfake creation. All required is a simple prompt, people have the opportunity to easily generate and publish their own deepfakes. 	Second, social media enable quick flows of information transmission. Compared to traditional newspapers, the Internet simplifies the process of sharing information – a post on X, or a short video on YouTube could soon achieve great focus. As deepfakes popularized, digital platforms paved the path to spread these AI-generated content. Therefore, circulation of deepfakes among the public might be attributed to Internet development.	Third, uneven and inadequate public cognition on deepfake plays a key part in its wide-spread and influence. Lacking knowledge in the frontmost deepfake technology makes it difficult to distinguish real videos and AI-generated content, tempting people to blindly credit misinformation and misunderstand political elites (Barari, et. al.). Consequences:	Wide spread of political deepfakes could lead to several consequences on countries and societies.	Firstly, political deepfakes break the balance of democratic elections. During election periods, partisan residents could spread deepfakes to criticize and disgrace political leaders. A deepfake of “Biden in a wheelchair” (Pierson) was made to suggest Biden was too old for becoming a president; Donald Trump under arrest (Pierson) was another example. Certain disinformation could mystify the public to some extent, possibly causing shifts in public views on election. Besides citizens, officials also committed manipulating deepfakes to gain advantage in elections. Indian politicians utilized deepfake phone calls, advertising people to vote for them. Hence, deepfake has effect of breaking election equity through manipulation of public opinions.	Secondly, political deepfakes weaken trust and exacerbate conflicts. In 2019 when conflict between India and Pakistan rose, deepfake videos involving demonstrations and marches by citizens from both countries, which irritated local nationalists and deteriorated international relationships (Liu). Both countries were obliged to temporarily close some social media platforms, preventing further amplification of turbulence.  Such social unrest caused by deepfakes represented how political deepfakes worsened relationship between nations.Courses of Action:To minimize the consequences of political deepfakes, two courses of actions are considered.First, governments should release policies and laws to restrain circulation of political deepfakes. Especially for instances in countries like India, where AI content significantly affected election fairness, local government could strictly implement rules to completely ban the exploitation of deepfake medias on national politics. The government could require digital platforms to detect deepfakes and possess rights to take down AI-generated content that breaks equity of democratic processes, therefore effectively reducing the impact of political deepfakes. However, this solution has potential drawback in its practicality. The government executes laws with too much force, in this case, mandatorily requiring digital platforms to detect and comprehensively eliminate the presence of deepfakes. Social platforms tend to reject coordination with the government and are possibly banned or restricted by the country. Hence, forceful policies couldn’t be applied on all social medias, so they aren’t the best choice to fight against political deepfakes. Another approach for governments resembles the deepfake policy published in China, “Implementation Outline for a Rule of Society,” which involves forcing to detect and label deepfakes on social media. This method is more practical since prevalent video platforms like Douyin (Wang) and BiliBili distinctly labeled AI-generated content, while national politics-related faked content isn’t permitted to be published, which prohibits impact of political deepfakes.  (The United Nation could also engage in drafting policies …)Second, enhancing public literacy on artificial intelligence also contributes to lessened influence of political deepfakes. Direct instructions are encouraged, such as lectures and meetings hosted in communities, or posters and advertisements introducing the concept of AI-generated content. Residents are informed by experts or experienced people about the typical characteristics of deepfakes, like unnatural facial movements and speaking tone (Rascius). As a result, people within local communities acquire skills and experience on distinguishing deepfakes and deceptions are less likely to appear. Certain method is less time-efficient and cost-effective. Making public speeches in town requires excessive labor and expenditure like inviting speakers and also occupies residents’ spare time. Consequently, direct instructions aren’t enough practical. An alternative approach is to prompt short videos that offer concise introduction about practical methods to discern between deepfakes and real information. Through social media, global citizens could actively consider and learn about the world’s newest technology by themselves, increasing awareness on misinformation from deepfakes. It’s more likely to observe changes from such approach, because digital platforms provide the medium for rapid information transfer, especially for short videos, so that more people see and learn via the Internet. Moreover, this method is less expensive as creating and watching such informational video takes short amounts of time only. Integrating the two courses of action most effectively reduces the impact of political deepfakes.  Inhibition of AI content on digital platforms provides insurance on its widespread, and improved literacy to discern deepfakes decreases misinformation on politics (Barari et. al). Conclusion:In conclusion, political deepfakes are an important global issue that requires active regulation, as they impact democratic practices and lowers trust among the public. Apart from technological detection of AI content, it is also important to foster public literacy and knowledge in political and digital technology. In China, deepfake detection is strictly prioritized for national security, while in United States, regulation of deepfakes focuses on election fairness and isn’t unified as a whole country. Caused by technological advancements and driven by social media platforms, political deepfakes need both strict restriction and improved public literacy in politics and digital technology.Personal Perspective:	The evidence and sources used in this report are mostly reliable and support the perspectives effectively. I referenced a formal academic research paper written by Barari and his colleagues. In the study, they conducted multiple experiments that applied detailed experimental techniques and made the examinations credible using statistical data. For example, “a nationally representative sample of 5,750 respondents” were included for the survey result, and two experiments, namely, “Exposure experiment” and “Detection experiment” were clearly recorded. This piece of evidence strongly supported one major global perspective that increased public literacy is considerable to limit the impact of deepfakes, because deepfakes affected no more than “equivalent misinformation conveyed through existing news formats.” I also used information from Wang’s paper which compared deepfake policies in United States and China. It was a credible academic journal source that provided formal citations. For instance, the author mentioned, “GAN technology made significant progress,” and referenced another published academic journal. The description of policies provided an objective view and elaborated my research by contrasting China and United States’ different national perspectives on how they chose to regulate deepfakes. Another source (Zhao) is published on Xinhua (news.cn), a influential news source in China that is responsible for posting authorized governmental news for the Communist Party of China and the country (基本情况_新华社).  Therefore, I regarded this source as reliable and suitable for my report. This news represented the national view from President Xi on the development of artificial intelligence, that the country considered ethics and discipline on technological innovation, supporting the strict policies related to deepfakes introduced later in the report. Furthermore, the evidence on India’s democratic election was a political review published on journal websites of Harvard University. The comment provided detailed information on the status quo of deepfakes manipulation during election in India with real-world examples. However, a limitation is that the journal potentially contained personal and biased political value, so perspectives from this source should be considered critically. Hence, I selected objective facts but avoided personal views.My perspective and understanding of the issue have been changed from others’ viewpoints and factual information. Before diving into research, I held profound interest on technology innovation and artificial intelligence and understood the use of deep learning algorithms to generate fake content. However, my knowledge was limited to seeing deepfake videos on digital platforms like BiliBili, and I noticed that the platform automatically detected and labeled AI contents. When researching, I also discovered that political deepfakes had been problematic in India’s elections, such as faked critiques on national leaders (Mani), which broadened my view on the negative impacts of technology with a real case. Additionally, seeing such severe impact of deepfakes that broke election equity, I wondered why I had never seen political deepfakes spreading via social media in China. But then I learned about restrictive laws and policies in China that strictly regulated deepfakes (Wang), which explained why BiliBili labeled AI-generated videos – China made detection a legal requirement. Moreover, previously I decided to answer my research question with a simple “yes,” that removal of political deepfakes should be legally required. Nonetheless, Rascius’s journal and Barari’s research supported a new stance that people should learn more about deepfake technology and politics to avoid deception and misinformation. This elaborated my thoughts and my perspective changed. Besides legal detection of political deepfakes, we should also promote wider understanding of technological knowledge.
